actions.thought: 4c 7s is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation from the button. It lacks connectivity and high-card value. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

In heads-up play, Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand well above the median winning hand. Being on the button gives me a positional advantage throughout the hand. Folding is out of the question. While limping is an option to keep the pot small, raising allows me to take initiative, potentially win the pot immediately if the opponent folds weak hands, or build value with a strong range advantage in position. A min-raise to 200 is a standard size at this stack depth (100bb), balancing pot control with aggression.

I am in the Big Blind and the Button has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. With 6h5c, I have a connector that plays reasonably well in a heads-up pot. The pot odds are 3-to-1 (100 to win 300), which is a very favorable price to defend my blind, even if I am out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am out of position on a paired board (3d 3h 7h) with only 6-high and very little equity. Standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. I have no reason to lead out here.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on a paired board of 3-3-7 with a flush draw. As the pre-flop raiser, I have a range advantage, but my specific hand has decent showdown value as Ace-high. Betting here folds out hands I beat (like King-high or Queen-high) and isolates me against hands that beat me (like a 7, a 3, or a pocket pair). By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and can comfortably call a bluff on the turn or value bet later if the board runs out safe. This protects my checking range.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, which completes a potential heart flush and is also a card that typically hits the pre-flop aggressor's range. However, the flop was checked through, suggesting neither of us has a particularly strong hand yet. I have the 6 of hearts, giving me a low flush draw. Checking is the most solid play here to see how my opponent reacts to the Ace. If they bet, I have the odds to potentially call with my flush draw, and if they check, I get a free card to the river.

The turn card is the Ace of Hearts, giving me top pair with a good kicker (Ac Ts) on a board of [3d 3h 7h Ah]. However, the board now contains three hearts, completing any flush draws. While I have improved to top pair, I do not hold a heart, making my hand vulnerable to flushes. My opponent has checked to me again. Betting here is thin for value because worse hands (like pocket pairs or a 7) may fold fearing the flush or the Ace, while better hands (flushes, trips, or two pair) will certainly continue or check-raise. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, avoid being blown off my equity by a raise, and potentially induce bluffs or extract value on a safe river card. This line minimizes variance on a wet, monotone board where I have good but not nutted showdown value.

I am out of position on the river with zero showdown value (6-high). The board is 3d 3h 7h Ah 9s. Both players checked the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the Ace or a flush (given I have the 6 of hearts), the pot is relatively small (400) and it is early in the tournament. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize losses, as any pair or even King-high from my opponent will beat me if the hand goes to showdown.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a board of [3d 3h 7h Ah 9s]. The board is somewhat wet with a potential flush (three hearts) and a pair of Threes. My opponent has checked to me on three consecutive streets, signaling a weak range that likely consists of missed draws, small pocket pairs, or weak one-pair hands like 7x or 9x. While my hand is likely the winner, betting for value is thin; worse hands will mostly fold, and better hands (like a flush, trips, or two pair) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity and win the pot against all bluffs and weaker made hands without risking a check-raise or value-owning myself on a dangerous board.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine of hearts, a strong starting hand in heads-up play that has great flush and straight potential. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value early with a hand that plays well post-flop.

I hold 4h 2d in the Big Blind against a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. 4-2 offsuit is extremely weak with very poor playability and equity. Although I'm getting 3:1 pot odds, being out of position with such a trash hand makes it difficult to realize any equity. Defending here is negative EV. The disciplined play is to fold.

I hold AJo on the button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With 100BB deep stacks, raising is the standard play to build the pot for value and take the initiative. I will open with a min-raise to 200, similar to my previous button strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 odds on a call, requiring roughly 25% equity. While K5o is a weak hand, it performs well enough against a typical Button opening range to justify a call and see a flop out of position. Folding would be too tight given the price, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength at this stage.

I have flopped bottom pair with a King kicker on a Queen-Nine-Five board with two clubs. While I have some showdown value, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Standard play in this spot is to check to the Button to see their continuation bet sizing and frequency. Donk betting here would likely only get called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me. I will check and plan to call most standard-sized continuation bets to see a turn.

I hold AdJh on a Qc9c5s board. I have Ace-high and am in position. The board texture connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, flush draws, straight draws). While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, my hand has showdown value that I want to protect, and I do not hold a club to block flush draws. Betting might fold out worse hands (King-high) but isolates me against better hands or strong draws, and facing a check-raise would be difficult. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and keeps my range wide. I will check.

The Turn card is the Jack of Spades, which is a very coordinated card that hits many parts of the Button's pre-flop raising range (KJ, QJ, JT, AJ, etc.). Since the flop went check-check, it is unlikely the opponent has a monster, but they could easily have improved to a better pair or a straight draw. My bottom pair (fives) has very little value for betting, and I am out of position. Checking again is the best move to control the pot and potentially reach a cheap showdown or see a safe river card.

On the Turn, the board is [Qc 9c 5s Js]. I have improved to second pair (Jacks) with a top kicker (Ace). The opponent has checked again. The board is becoming increasingly coordinated with potential straights (KT, T8) and two flush draws present. While I have showdown value, betting here is thin; worse hands might fold, and better hands (Qx, straights, two pair) will call or raise. If I get check-raised, I would likely have to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid difficult spots, and induce bluffs on the river or comfortably call a value bet from a worse hand later. I will check to get to showdown cheaply.

I have improved to two pair (Kings and Fives) on the river. However, the board is very dangerous as any Ten makes a straight (9-10-J-Q-K). Given that both the flop and turn were checked through, the opponent's range is wide. Betting might only get called by hands that beat me (straights or better two pairs like KQ or KJ) or a Queen that might have checked down. By checking, I can minimize losses if the opponent has a straight and potentially induce a bluff from missed club draws or weaker pairs. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I hold AdJh on a board of Qc9c5sJsKs. I have a pair of Jacks, which is now third pair. The river King is a significant scare card as it brings a possible flush (3 spades on board) and completes a 4-card straight (9-J-Q-K) where any Ten makes a straight. The opponent has checked to me on all three streets. Given the board texture, it is unlikely a worse hand calls a bet (worse hands like 9x or 5x likely fold due to the K and straight/flush potential), and better hands (Kx, Qx, Tx, flush) will certainly call or raise. I have showdown value against busted club draws or weak pairs that the opponent has checked down. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity without risking chips in a spot where I am rarely called by worse.

I have Ace-Trey suited on the Button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot, following my previous aggressive pre-flop patterns.

I hold J4o in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the opponent on the Button. While heads-up ranges are wide, J4o is in the bottom tier of starting hands with poor playability and reverse implied odds. Against a 3x sizing, calling is less attractive than against a min-raise due to the worse price (calling 200 to win 600 vs calling 100 to win 400) and the difficulty of playing this hand out of position. Since it is early in the match with deep stacks, there is no need to defend this weak holding.

I hold Qh 2s (Q2 offsuit) in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand with poor playability due to the low kicker and lack of flush potential. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, Q2o is typically near the bottom of the playable range and is frequently a standard fold, especially this early in the tournament where there is no need to force marginal spots. My opponent has been passive post-flop but raising buttons, so I don't have a specific read to exploit with a weak hand. Folding saves chips.

I am on the button with 3s6s, a speculative suited hand. In heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is key. Previous hands indicate that the opponent has folded to 3x raises pre-flop. I will continue this strategy to pick up the blinds or take control of the pot early.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4d facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the opponent on the Button/SB. The pot odds are 200 to call for a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. While 84s has raw equity close to 37% against a wide heads-up opening range, the larger raise size (3bb vs the standard 2bb) makes the price to call worse, and realizing equity out of position with a weak suited two-gapper is difficult. I have folded to this sizing twice previously with worse hands, and while this is suited, it is still marginal. Conserving chips and waiting for a slightly better spot to defend (like connected hands or high cards) is prudent early in the match.

In heads-up play, K8o is a standard opening hand from the button (SB), as it ranks well within the top 50% of hands. Folding here would be too passive. My previous raises (with ATo, AJo) were to 200, and the opponent has defended the Big Blind by calling. A min-raise allows me to maintain initiative and position while risking a small amount to potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Limping might invite an aggressive raise from the opponent, which would be uncomfortable. Therefore, I will raise to 200 to balance my opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with 84o. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. While the price to call is only 100 more into a 300 pot (offering 3:1 pot odds), 84o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. In a heads-up match, defending wide is necessary, but 84o is near the bottom of my range. I have already called several min-raises in previous hands with better holdings (65c, K5h). Folding here keeps my stack healthy and avoids playing a marginal hand out of position against a player who seems to be opening a standard range.

A9o is a strong hand heads-up, especially on the button. I have position and a hand that is well ahead of the opponent's range. I will raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and put pressure on the opponent.

I hold King-Ten suited (Kd Td) in the Big Blind facing a 3x open raise (300 chips) from the Button. This is a strong heads-up hand that is well ahead of a typical Button opening range. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a pot of 600, offering excellent odds for a hand with this playability and high-card strength. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and while a 3-bet is a viable option to seize initiative, calling is a lower-variance play that keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize equity post-flop from out of position.

I have flopped trip Kings (Kd Ks Kc) with a Ten kicker on a board of [4c Ks Kc]. This is a monster hand in a heads-up scenario. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard optimal play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely cause the opponent to fold their air hands, whereas checking induces them to make a continuation bet with a wide range (including bluffs and draws). My goal is to extract maximum value, and checking is the best way to allow the opponent to put more chips in the pot with a weaker range.

On a paired board of 4c-Ks-Kc, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture favors my range, which contains many Kings. I will place a continuation bet of 1/3 the pot (200 chips) to fold out the opponent's air and low equity hands, while also setting up to represent a strong hand on later streets.

I hold Trip Kings with a Ten kicker on a K-K-4 board. My opponent has c-bet 200 (1/3 pot) after I checked. Raising here (check-raising) would likely force folds from all of the opponent's air and weak hands, which constitutes a large portion of their range in a heads-up match. Calling is superior because it keeps their bluffing range wide and disguises the strength of my hand, allowing me to extract value on later streets. I am well ahead of almost everything except a better King, which is statistically unlikely. The flush draw on board is a minor concern, but not enough to justify blowing the pot up immediately and losing value from bluffs.

I have improved to a Full House (Kings full of Fours) on the Turn [4c Ks Kc 4d]. With the board double-paired, I am now beating any flush or straight potential, and only losing to better Kings (KJ+) or Quads (44, K4). My hand is effectively the nuts against the vast majority of the opponent's range. Leading out (donk betting) here would look very strong and likely fold out all of the opponent's bluffs and air. Checking is the superior play to allow the opponent to continue bluffing or value betting a worse King or pocket pair. I am not vulnerable to free cards since a flush would still lose to my full house. Therefore, I will check to induce further action.

The turn board of 4c-Ks-Kc-4d is double-paired. I have Ace-high (A9), which has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot. My opponent called a small flop bet on a K-K-4 board, suggesting they might have a piece of it or a pocket pair. By checking back on the turn, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to reach the river for free. If my opponent is floating with a worse hand, I am still ahead, and if they have a King or a Four, I avoid losing more chips to a hand that is unlikely to fold. Checking back is the most balanced and safe play here to realize my equity.

I hold a powerful Full House, Kings full of Tens (Kd Td on 4c Ks Kc 4d Th), which is effectively the nuts as I beat any King, any Ten, and any lower full house; only Quad Fours (44) beats me. My opponent's check-back on the Turn strongly suggests they lack a King and likely hold a marginal hand like Ace-high or a pocket pair. On this double-paired board, pocket pairs 55-99 are counterfeited and merely 'play the board' (KK44T), meaning they will likely fold to any bet. However, Ace-high hands (KK44A) beat the board and are my primary target for value. Checking is risky as the opponent showed weakness on the Turn and will likely check back the River. Therefore, I will make a small value bet of 400 (40% pot) to encourage a call from Ace-high or better pocket pairs (JJ+).

The board is double-paired (Kings and Fours) with a Ten on the river. My hand, Ace-Nine, yields the best possible two-pair combination: Kings and Fours with an Ace kicker (K-K-4-4-A). I only lose to a King or a Four, which would make a full house. Pro's lead on the river for 400 into a 1000 pot could represent a King, but it could also be a value bet with a Ten (which I beat) or a bluff. Given the 4.5:1 pot odds and the fact that I have the 'nut' non-full-house hand, calling is the most profitable decision to realize my showdown value. Raising is unnecessary as I would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

I hold Qh 7h on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a respectable starting hand (suited Queen), well within the top 50% of hands, making it suitable for an open. My opponent has shown a tendency to call min-raises from the Big Blind but plays passively post-flop (checking down frequently). Raising to 200 allows me to take the initiative, utilize my position, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or navigate effectively post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 94o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop, which gives me 3:1 pot odds. However, 94o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. I previously folded 84o in a similar situation (Hand #8). At this early stage of the tournament, I should prioritize post-flop playability and avoid defending the bottom of my range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q7o is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play, representing the top half of my range. I have successfully stolen the blinds with a 3x raise in previous hands (#3, #5, #7), and I will maintain this sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Opponent (Flash) is on the Button/SB and has raised to 300 (3bb). This appears to be their standard opening size, and they have been active, raising 5 out of the last 6 times they had the button, suggesting a very wide opening range. In a Heads-Up match, the Big Blind must defend a wide range of hands against a frequent opener to prevent being exploited. My hand, King-Deuce suited (K2c), has decent playability and equity (approx 53% vs a random hand). Although I will be out of position post-flop which reduces equity realization, the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400, needing 33% equity) combined with the strength of a suited King against a wide range make this a standard defend (Call). Folding would be too tight given the opponent's frequency.

I have flopped top pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (Deuce) on a K-T-4 board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard line is to check. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out worse hands that I want to keep in the pot (bluffs, lower pairs) and isolate me against better Kings or strong draws. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet, but not strong enough to build a large pot immediately. I will check to see how the opponent proceeds.

On a King-high board with a Ten, I hold Queen-high with some backdoor potential. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will fire a continuation bet of 200 into the 600 pot. This sizing is designed to fold out weaker hands and air while keeping the pot manageable if I am called, utilizing the board's texture which favors my perceived opening range.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a fairly coordinated board (K-T-4 with a flush draw). My opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to open wide and c-bet frequently. Their bet size is small (1/3 pot), which is a standard c-bet size for a wide range. I am getting excellent pot odds (4:1) to call. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (better Kings, Two Pair, Sets) while folding out the bluffs I am currently beating. Folding is far too tight given the strength of my hand in a heads-up scenario. The best play is to call to keep their bluffing range in and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated (4h Kd Th 9c). The Turn 9c completes a straight for QJ and gives two pair to hands like T9 or K9, though these are specific holdings. In a Heads-Up match, Top Pair is still a strong hand that I want to take to showdown, but my weak kicker makes it vulnerable to value bets from better Kings. Leading out (donking) is generally poor strategy here as it allows the opponent to play perfectly—folding worse hands and continuing with better. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size, induce bluffs from missed draws or weaker holdings, and evaluate the opponent's action. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet or check-down to the river.

On this turn, the 9c is a card that could have potentially improved the opponent's range (J8, T9, or a pair of 9s). I have a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (a Jack would give me K-Q-J-T-9). Since Pro called the flop bet, they likely have a King or a Ten. By checking back, I take a free card to realize my equity and avoid a potential check-raise, while keeping the pot manageable for a possible bluff on the river if I miss and the board texture changes.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) on a very wet board (K-T-4-9-J). The river Jack completes potential straights for any Queen (Qx) and improves hands like KJ, QJ, J9, or JT to two pair or better. My kicker (Deuce) does not play, as the board's J-T-9 are the active kickers for any King without an Ace. This means I chop with any non-straight King worse than AK. Given the opponent checked back the turn, their range is capped, likely containing marginal made hands or draws. Betting here is thin value; worse hands (Tx, 9x) might fold due to the scary board, and better hands (Straights, Two Pair) will call or raise. Checking allows me to see a cheap showdown or evaluate a bet from the opponent, keeping their bluffing range alive.

On the river, the Jack completes a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K) for my Queen. While an Ace-Queen would make a higher Ace-high straight, it is unlikely given Pro's check on the river. Pro's line (calling pre-flop and flop, checking turn and river) suggests a marginal made hand like a King, a Ten, or a Jack, or possibly another Queen for a split pot. I will bet 600 into the 1000 pot to extract value from these weaker made hands.

The river is the Jack of Diamonds, making the final board 4h Kd Th 9c Jd. This is a very dangerous board as any Queen makes a straight (9-T-J-Q-K). Additionally, the Jack connects with many hands in the opponent's range that would check the turn, such as QJ, JT, or J9, making two pair. My hand, King-Deuce, is effectively just top pair with the board playing the kickers (K-K-J-T-9). I lose to any Two Pair (K4, K9, KT, KJ), any Set, any Straight (Qx), and any King with an Ace kicker. I chop with K3-K8. The opponent bet 600 into 1000 after checking the turn. Given Flash's history of playing strong hands passively (Hand #9 check-called river with Trips), a significant river bet here indicates strength, likely a straight or better two pair. My hand is at the very bottom of my value range and essentially a bluff-catcher. I have many better hands in my Big Blind defense range (Qx, Two Pair) to call with. Folding is the disciplined decision.

I am on the Button (SB) with K6o. This is a reasonable hand for heads-up play, holding a King blocker and being above the median hand strength. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to min-raises in previous hands (Hand #8, #10). Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with initiative. Folding is too tight, and limping gives a free flop to the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Button has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. While the pot odds are 3-to-1, this hand is very weak and plays poorly out of position. I have previously folded similar hands like 84s and 94o to this sizing. Maintaining a disciplined range from the blinds is important to avoid losing chips in marginal post-flop situations.

I am on the button with Jc 6d. In heads-up play, J6o is a hand worth playing, especially considering that 'Pro' has shown a tendency to fold to my pre-flop raises from the small blind in several previous hands (3, 5, and 7). I will raise to 300 to continue applying pressure and maintain my aggressive image, which has been successful in picking up pots pre-flop.

I hold A9s in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play, having significant equity against a standard button opening range. My opponent (Flash) has raised to 3bb (300). Being out of position post-flop, a 3-bet could bloat the pot and isolate me against a stronger range or face a 4-bet, forcing a tough decision. Calling allows me to keep their range wide (including hands I dominate like Kx, Qx, and worse Ax) and realize my equity at a good price (calling 200 to win a pot of 600).

I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah9h) on a Kh Jh 7d board. Out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard and most effective line. This board texture (two broadway cards) connects well with the opponent's opening range, making a 'donk bet' (leading out) unwise as it folds out their bluffs and isolates me against their stronger holdings. By checking, I allow the opponent to c-bet, at which point I can decide whether to check-call to realize equity or check-raise to apply pressure given my strong draw.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) on a board of Kd-Jh-7d. There are straight and flush draws available, and a King is a very likely card for the Big Blind's calling range. However, middle pair is strong in heads-up play. Since 'Pro' checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from draws or weaker hands like middle/bottom pairs, and to gain information on the strength of my opponent's hand.

I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah9h) on a Kh Jh 7d board. My opponent has bet 300 (half pot) into a pot of 600. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win 900 immediately, total pot 1200), which requires 25% equity. A flush draw has approximately 19% equity to hit on the next card and ~36% by the river, plus I have an Ace overcard which could win if I pair it against smaller pairs. Check-raising is an option but risks bloating the pot out of position against a range that connects very well with this board (Kings and Jacks). Calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize my equity and keep the opponent's bluffing range wide.

I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah9h) on a Kh Jh 7d 6s board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop, the standard line is to check. The turn card (6s) is a brick that does not change the board texture significantly or improve my hand. Leading out (donk betting) here is not advisable as the board heavily favors the opponent's range (Kings, Jacks), and I would likely only be called by hands that beat me or raised by strong value. By checking, I hope to see a free river card to realize my equity or face a bet that I can evaluate based on pot odds. My goal is to hit the flush on the river while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

The turn 6s significantly improves my hand to two pair (Jacks and Sixes). In a heads-up match, this is an extremely strong holding. Pro checked again after calling my flop continuation bet, which suggests they likely have a King, a draw, or a weaker Jack. I will bet 600 into the 1200 pot to extract value from King-high hands and to charge drawing hands (like heart flushes or straight draws with QT/T9) to see the river. Checking back might allow a draw to hit for free or miss out on a street of value from a King.

On the turn, the pot is 1200 and the opponent bets 600 (half pot). I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah9h) with the board reading 7d Jh Kh 6s. I am facing a bet of 600 to win a total pot of 2400, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). My direct equity to hit a flush on the river is approximately 20% (9 outs). While my direct equity is slightly below the required pot odds, the implied odds of holding the nut flush justify the call, as I can extract significant value on the river if a heart hits. Additionally, my Ace high has some minimal showdown value against missed straight draws. Folding the nut flush draw for a half-pot bet is too weak, and raising isolates me against better hands. Calling is the optimal play.

I hold the Nut Flush (Ah9h) on a board of [7d Jh Kh 6s 2h]. The river card completes the flush draw I've been chasing. Having check-called the flop and turn, checking again is the most deceptive and theoretically sound play. It allows the opponent (who has been the aggressor on all streets) to continue bluffing with missed straight draws or value bet with hands like sets, two pair, or strong top pairs. If the opponent bets, I can check-raise for maximum value. Leading out risks folding out their bluffs and makes my hand strength too transparent.

I have two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on a board of 7d-Jh-Kh-6s-2h. While two pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play, the river 2h is a potential scare card as it completes the heart flush draw that was present since the flop. Pro has check-called every street, which is consistent with either a King-high hand (Top Pair) or a draw that may have just hit. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and win against all of Pro's one-pair hands (like AK, KQ, KT). Betting might extract more value from a King, but it also risks a check-raise from a completed flush or a superior two-pair (like KJ or K7). Checking is the safest way to realize my hand's significant showdown value.

I hold 3d 5c on the Button (SB), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Heads-Up Texas Hold'em. Despite having the positional advantage post-flop, the hand lacks playability and equity. My opponent (Flash) has shown aggression when entering pots but has also folded to my pre-flop raises in the Big Blind. However, attempting to steal with 5-3 offsuit is unnecessary risk given my current chip lead and the early stage of the tournament. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My standard opening size throughout the session has been 3x (300), and I will continue with that sizing to maintain consistency and put pressure on the big blind.

The opponent (Flash) has been consistently raising to 300 (3x) from the button. While Q2s has some equity and is suited, defending out of position against a 3x raise with a weak Queen and a bad kicker is generally a losing play in the long run due to reverse implied odds and difficulty realizing equity. I have folded similar weak hands (84s, J4o) to this sizing before. I will preserve my stack lead and fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K7o, which is a playable hand in heads-up poker, usually ranked in the top 40% of hands.
